title: Carbon Nanotube and Polypyrrole Composites: Coating and Doping
abstract:
Communications
522
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0704-0522 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 2000, 12, No. 7
the plane surface and the diffusion rate decreases with de-
creasing pore size. With decreasing pore size, the pH gradi-
ent at the bottom of the pore, which is imposed by the elec-
troreduction of protons, increases. Therefore, the growth
rate of the nanowire is enhanced as the pore size decreases.
In conclusion, we have investigated the growth of single-
crystal CdS nanowires using electrochemically induced de-
position in the pores of an AAO template from acidic
chemical baths containing CdCl2 and TAA. TEM and
HREM investigations indicated that these nanowires have
a uniform hexagonal CdS single-crystal structure and the
{100} surfaces of the CdS crystals are parallel to the axes of
the nanowires. The above results imply that the growth of
the nanowire may be influenced profoundly by different
electrochemical processes.
Experimental
The AAO templates with pore sizes of about 8, 20, and 90 nm were
grown by potentiostatic anodization of high-purity aluminum plates
(0.15 mm thick, 99.9+ % purity) in aqueous solutions of 14 % H2SO4 and
4 % and 2 % oxalic acid, respectively, at 0±20 C. After the anodization, the
remaining aluminum was etched by a 20 % HCl±0.1 M CuCl2 mixed solu-
tion. Then the barrier layer was dissolved using 20 % H2SO4. Finally, a silver
film was deposited by vacuum evaporation onto one surface of the template
membrane to provide a conductive contact.
The deposition of CdS was performed potentiostatically at a potential
value of ±0.65 V referred to the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) in a
three-electrode configuration in a glass cell at 70 C by immersing the cell in
a water bath for 5±8 h. A platinum plate, SCE, and the Ag/AAO substrate
were used as counter, reference, and working electrodes, respectively. The
electrolyte solution consisted of 0.05 M CdCl2 and 0.10 M TAA, as in the
work by Yamaguchi et al. [25]. In order to avoid the erosion of the template,
the initial pH of this mixture was adjusted to 4.6 by adding appropriate
amounts of HCl. In all cases, the electrolyte was freshly prepared and
heated for 1 h at 70 C prior to each deposition. After the deposition, the
nanowires in the AAO templates were ultrasonically washed in water for
1 min, rinsed with double distilled water, and finally dried in air at room
temperature before being subjected to further analysis.
AAO was completely removed from the Ag/AAO/CdS samples by
mounting the foil on a glass using epoxy resin and then dissolving the AAO
template in 1 M NaOH at 25 C for 1 h and washing several times with dou-
ble distilled water. For transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the nano-
wires were detached from the substrate by being ultrasonically dispersed in
1 cm3 water, then a small drop of the solution was placed on a Cu grid cov-
ered with carbon film. A transmission electron microscope, JEM-200CX,
operated at 160 keV and fitted with X-ray energy dispersion analysis equip-
ment (EDAX 9100/6, Philips), was employed to study the morphology of
the nanowires. For the high-resolution electron microscopy (HREM) obser-
vations, a JEOL-2010 electron microscope was employed at 300 kV at room
temperature. Raman spectra were directly measured on a bulk nanowire
sample using 514.5 nm laser light from a He:Ne laser and a Renishaw Sys-
tem 1000 Raman microscope.

Final version: January 12, 2000

Carbon Nanotube and Polypyrrole Composites:
Coating and Doping**
By George Z. Chen,* Milo S. P. Shaffer, Dan Coleby,
Gemma Dixon, Wuzong Zhou, Derek J. Fray,
and Alan H. Windle
This work focuses on the combination of the complemen-
tary properties of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and polypyr-
role (PPy), following an electrochemical route to the for-
mation of a novel composite material. Electrochemical
techniques and microscopy were used to demonstrate un-
precedentedly the anionic dopant behavior of the nano-
tubes within the polymer films, and to indicate how the
presence of nanotubes can improve the conductivity. High-
resolution electron microscopy (HREM) revealed the indi-
vidual nanotubes to be uniformly coated with PPy, which
formed bridges between the nanotubes to give dense com-
posite films.
The remarkable mechanical and electrical properties ex-
hibited by CNTs have encouraged efforts to develop mass
production techniques.[1,2] As a result, CNTs are becoming
increasingly available, and more attention from both aca-
demia and industry is focused on the applications of CNTs
±
[*] Dr. G. Z. Chen, M. S. P. Shaffer, D. Coleby, G. Dixon,
Prof. D. J. Fray, Prof. A. H. Windle
Department of Materials Science and Metallurgy
University of Cambridge
Pembroke Street, Cambridge CB2 3QZ (UK)
Dr. W. Zhou
Department of Chemistry, University of St. Andrews
St. Andrews, Fife, KY16 9ST (UK)
[**] The authors gratefully acknowledge the assistance from I. Kinloch
with some of the TEM investigations.
 in bulk quantities. These opportunities include the use of
CNTs as a conductive filler in insulating polymer ma-
trices,[3] and as a reinforcement in structural materials.[4]
Other potential applications exploit the size of CNTs as a
template to grow nanosized, and hence ultra-high surface-
to-volume ratio catalysts,[5] or aim to combine CNTs to
form nanoelectronic elements.
On the other hand, electronically conducting polymers
(ECPs) have been the focus of many intensive research pro-
grams over the past two decades.[6±11] Simple ECPs, typical-
ly PPy, polyaniline, and polythiophene, can be prepared
either chemically in a bulk quantity, or electrochemically as
a thin film. In addition to a relatively high conductivity in
oxidized and doped states, simple ECPs show interesting
physicochemical properties exploitable for batteries, sen-
sors, light-emitting diodes, and electrochromic displays.[6]
Furthermore, there are two additional opportunities that al-
low the functionality of simple ECPs to be extended. Firstly,
large anions with particular functions, such as natural en-
zymes or catalytic transition metal complexes, can be used
as the counter anion/dopant and therefore can be entrapped
within the ECP matrix[7,8] during the polymerization pro-
cess. Secondly, the monomers of conventional ECPs are a
class of versatile molecular building blocks suitable for the
synthesis of various more complicated, functionalized
monomers that can, for example, be polymerized to form
sensory devices aimed at molecular recognition.[9±11]
However, both CNTs and ECPs generate particular chal-
lenges in some materials applications. For example, in order
to use CNTs efficiently as a filler material, either for con-
ductive or mechanical purposes, sufficiently high loadings
must be achieved within the composite to enable percola-
tion. In the specific case of the use of CNTs as nano-
electronic elements, one of the difficult tasks will be to at-
tach them both to each other and to an external electronic
framework. On the other hand, all known simple ECPs are
mechanically weak and have to be oxidized and doped by a
counter anion to achieve significant conductivity. The
strength of a conducting polymer may be improved by, for
example, copolymerization with a second polymer such as
poly(vinylchloride) (PVC) but a sacrifice in conductivity is
inevitable.[12] In addition, because dopants constitute a large
proportion of conducting polymers, typically 20±40 vol.-%,
and all the dopants used so far are themselves insulators,
the overall conductivity of ECPs is somewhat limited.[7] Re-
tardant effects of some inorganic dopants on the optical
properties of ECPs have also been reported.[3] Further-
more, in a practical application in a reducing environment,
a conducting polymer material with a non-conductive do-
pant may lose its conductivity altogether.
The methodology employed in the current study has
been to grow a conducting polymer film on an electrode
surface using ionized (anionic) CNTs as the dopant. The
arc-grown CNTs used were supplied by Dynamic Enter-
prises (DEL). Examination under the transmission electron
microscope (TEM) demonstrated that the starting material
contained about 10±30 vol.-% of straight CNTs, with the
remainder being mainly carbon nanoparticles and amor-
phous carbon. The DEL sample was chemically oxidized
and dispersed in water, resulting in a black and slightly
acidic aqueous suspension; at this point, the surfaces of the
CNTs were covered with oxygen-containing groups such as
carboxylic acids.[3,13,14] The suspension tolerated a weak
electrolyte strength (about 10±3 M or lower) and a change
in pH from 3 to 7. It could be diluted readily but drying
caused irreversible solidification.
Pyrrole was chosen as a suitable monomer because it can
be polymerized in a neutral aqueous solution[6±11] in which
the CNT suspensions were stable. The concentration of
CNTs (0.001±0.5 wt.-%) in the electrochemical solution
was
adjusted
by
dilution
with
the
pyrrole
solution
(0.1±0.5 M). No additional supporting electrolyte was used
in order to avoid the involvement of any dopant other than
the ionized CNTs. For the electrochemical experiments, a
simple three-electrode and one apartment cell was used in
an ambient environment. Argon was used to degas and
protect the electrochemical solution. Gold, platinum, tita-
nium, copper, and vitreous carbon, but mostly graphite in
various shapes, were used as the working electrode. A
graphite rod (6.5 mm diameter) and a saturated Calomel
electrode (SCE) were used as the counter and reference
electrodes, respectively.
Electropolymerization was carried out using either con-
stant or cyclic potential, or constant current electrolysis
with the monomer oxidation potential being set between
0.7 and 1.0 V against SCE. As indicated by an increase in
current with electrolysis time and by the formation of a
black coating, the polymerization occurred when the pyr-
role concentration was relatively high, 0.1±0.5 M. This re-
sult suggested that the carbon nanotube suspension acted
as a weak supporting electrolyte. Furthermore, an increase
in CNT concentration accelerated the growth of the poly-
mer coating, demonstrating that CNTs indeed participated
in the electrolysis. As in the case of simple ECPs, the com-
posite coating grew faster when the oxidation potential was
increased. No coating was observed during electrolysis of a
CNT suspension in the absence of pyrrole.
In comparison to pure PPy films formed under similar
conditions using 0.001±0.5 M KCl as a source of counter
ions, the composite films were notably less brittle and more
adhesive to the electrode. After electrolysis, the composite
film coated electrode was rinsed in water and then trans-
ferred into a pure 0.1±0.5 M KCl solution, and cyclic vol-
tammograms recorded (Fig. 1); the composite films showed
most notably much less hysteresis than the pure PPy films.
This behavior of the composite films was not significantly
affected by the polymerization conditions employed. Hys-
teresis, characterized by a sharp oxidation wave at a rela-
tively positive potential and a broad and flat reduction
wave, is a common feature observed on cyclic voltammo-
grams of simple ECPs. Although the mechanism is com-
plex,[15] it can be rationalized by considering that an oxi-

ent at the bottom of the pore, which is imposed by the elec-

In conclusion, we have investigated the growth of single-
crystal CdS nanowires using electrochemically induced de-
position in the pores of an AAO template from acidic
chemical baths containing CdCl2 and TAA. TEM and
HREM investigations indicated that these nanowires have
a uniform hexagonal CdS single-crystal structure and the
{100} surfaces of the CdS crystals are parallel to the axes of
the nanowires. The above results imply that the growth of
the nanowire may be influenced profoundly by different

dized ECP is electronically conducting along the polymer
chains and structurally open to the movement of charge-
balancing counter ions between the chains; whereas a re-
duced ECP has the opposite properties. Therefore, when a
reduced ECP is being oxidized, polarization occurs due to
the resistivity of the polymer to electron withdrawal and to
the intercalation of counter ions; on the other hand, the re-
duction of an oxidized ECP is more thermodynamically
controlled. In the composite films, however, the polariza-
tion caused by electron transfer and counter anion interca-
lation is reduced. The decrease in electron transfer polar-
ization is expected because the electron flow in the
reduced composite film can be aided by the entrapped
CNTs. Furthermore, unlike previous work on CNT±poly-
mer composites,[3] our CNT-PPy composites were formed
using relatively immobile CNTs as the charge balancing an-
ionic dopant during polymerization of PPy. In the reduced
state, the PPy chains become neutral and the negative
charge on the physically entrapped large and anionic CNTs
must be balanced by a smaller charge cation. Firstly, the
presence of the cations in the reduced composite polymer
film makes it less compact than traditional ECP films, thus
easing the movement of ions. More importantly, the oxida-
tion of the polymer is now accompanied by the expulsion
of the smaller cations, rather than anion intercalation, and
hence ion-transfer polarization is evaded.[7,8,16]
Further supportive evidence for this hypothesis was ob-
tained when electrochemical impedance spectra of the
composite films were recorded in 0.1±0.5 M KCl (Fig. 2).
In comparison to similarly prepared pure PPy films
(Fig. 2), the composite films exhibited only diffusive (high
frequency) and capacitive (low frequency) behavior re-
gardless of the oxidation state. This diffusive±capacitive
feature is typical of an oxidized thin ECP film,[15,16] but has
never been observed for a reduced ECP either with or
without entrapped large anions. We can therefore conclude
that our composite films are conducting even in a reduced
state, a result that can only be attributed to the presence of
CNTs within the composite films (in good agreement with
the cyclic voltammetry observations).
After the film was rinsed in water and dried in a vacuum
box at room temperature, it was inspected using optical
and high-resolution electron microscopy. As expected, a
significant difference between the composite and pure PPy
films was only revealed by high-resolution scanning elec-
tron microscopy (SEM). This approach confirmed the pres-
ence of CNTs within the films and demonstrated the for-
mation of dense, high-quality composite films. In addition,
the microscopy of the composite films did not show an ob-
vious relation, except in extreme cases, between the con-
Fig. 1. Cyclic voltammograms comparing the electrochemical hysteresis of a
pure PPy film (thinner line) to that of a CNT±PPy composite film (thicker
line) in 0.5 M KCl. Both films were grown under argon onto a graphite disk
(6.5 mm diameter) by passing a constant current (5 mA) for 100 s. The solu-
tions for electrochemical polymerization contained 0.5 M KCl and 0.5 M
pyrrole for growing the pure PPy film, and 0.2 wt.-% CNTs and 0.5 M pyr-
role for growing the CNT±PPy composite film.
Fig. 2. Electrochemical impedance spectra demonstrating the difference in
conductive behavior between pure PPy and CNT±PPy composite films that
were a) partially oxidized, b) partially reduced, and c) fully reduced. The
polymer films and the electrolyte solution were the same as those for Fig-
centration of CNTs in the electrochemical solution and that
in the resulting composite film. This observation is actually
in accordance with the proposed dopant role of CNTs, i.e.,
their concentration in the film is determined by the total
charge on the PPy chains. Further work is needed to verify
whether CNTs have been incorporated into the film by
other mechanisms during the polymerization process.
Nevertheless, there are some microscopic features that are
worthy of mention (Fig. 3). Firstly, there was no significant
alignment of CNTs within the film, but there were areas
with localized enrichment of CNTs relative to the nano-
tube-to-particle ratio in the original DEL product; sec-
ondly, careful inspections of the thickness and surface tex-
ture of the nanotubes suggested that there must be a
polymer coating on the surface of each nanotube; finally,
many neighboring CNTs were joined together by conduct-
ing polymer at a variety of angles.
Fig. 3. Scanning electron micrograph of the surface of a CNT±PPy film
(same as Fig. 1), showing a relatively rare section of 1) a nanotube that had
not been coated by the end of the experiment, 2) a fully coated nanotube,
3) a coated nanoparticle, and 4) a join between a coated nanotube and an-
other coated nanoparticle or nanotube.
The SEM findings stimulated our curiosity as to how the
polymer coating was formed on the outer surface of individ-
ual CNTs. The coating on the nanotubes in the above-men-
tioned composite films is too thick (>100 nm) to be in-
spected by TEM. Therefore, by electrolysis at a low
potential for a short time (see Fig. 4 for details), a tiny
amount of the composite was grown on a bare copper grid
that was suspended on a platinum wire. Upon TEM imag-
ing, nanotubes were observed both enclosed in and protrud-
ing from the edges of the bulk composite film. On these pro-
truding nanotubes an amorphous coating was observed
(Fig. 4) that was much thicker and more uniform than the
disturbance (<1 nm) on the outer surface of CNTs exam-
ined after oxidation. This coating can only be attributed to a
remarkably uniform layer of PPy. Because the coating ob-
served in these shorter, low potential experiments is much
thinner (5±10 nm) than that seen in the earlier experiments
(50 nm), there is an implication that the thickness of the
coating could be controllable. We were again interested by
the observation that these protruding nanotubes were
joined to other nanotubes by means of the polymer (Fig. 4).
Finally, we also mention here an interesting phenomenon
observed during this work. After electropolymerization,
the used pyrrole and CNT suspensions were kept in a
small, sealed beaker. When these solutions were checked
again about a month later they had gelled. High-resolution
SEM and TEM examinations of small amounts of these
gels indicated the presence of polymeric material between
the nanotubes, which almost certainly acts as a crosslinking
agent. To our knowledge, until now no gels have been
formed from carbon nanotube suspensions with a concen-
tration less than 0.5 wt.-%, nor has a PPy gel been pre-
In summary, we have reported here: 1) a novel electro-
chemical method for the synthesis of a CNT±PPy compos-
ite that has a high concentration of well-dispersed nano-
tubes that are wetted by the continuous polymer phase.
This study is the first example of anionic CNTs acting as a
strong and conductive dopant in the polymerization of a
conducting polymer; (after this report was submitted, two
recent short reports[17,18] were brought to our attention dis-
cussing composites of CNTs and conducting polymers, al-
though the CNTs were not used as the electrolyte). 2) The
preparation of a remarkably uniform PPy coating on indi-
vidual CNTs, which promises controlled modification of
the outer surface of CNTs to provide selectable functional-
ities. 3) The observation of the joining of individual CNTs
by conducting PPy, which may have potential applications
in CNT-based nanoelectronic structures.
Final version: December 13, 1999
